posts.name: Matthew King
posts.cooked:
Mugneret Gibourg, Clos Vougeot, 2006

Forget the rubbish about “off” vintages. This wine is flat-out great. It has the weight, density and breeding that shouts Grand Cru, but it whispers with amazing delicacy as well. It’s a yin yang wine – perfectly poised between masculine and feminine traits. Sinewy power with effortless grace.

After 30 minutes of air, I was reveling in the wine’s airy, dark cherry profile. After two hours, it took on serious flesh and stony weight. It just kept growing in the glass. Very polished and smooth, as if someone had gently buffed out the wine on a jewel sander. Balanced acidity frames the distinctive soil notes and fruit, with flecks of Vosne-like spice to keep it lively. Terrific mouthfeel.

I think this is just at the start of its drinking window and has room for further nuances to come with age.

I’m always amazed that I never seem to have an off or shut down bottle from M-G. Their portfolio is varied but oh so consistent. I know I’m gushing a bit … but I love the sisters Gibourg. And I love how many 06s are drinking now.

2006 “off”?

+1
I almost would have posted the same.

I guess there are some people who think only in termes of “great vintage” … and the rest is “off” …
certainly due to a lack of experience and knowledge …

Real off-vintages are nowadays more rare than great vintages … and there are a lot of “non-great vintages” that have a great deal of virtues: sooner drinkable, less structured, often with vg balance and less dramatic, fine with food, cheaper …

While I would consider a 2006 CV being still a bit on the young side … I would defnitely prefer it to a 2005 (NOW !)

posts.name: David Bueker

posts.name: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)
posts.cooked:
James Billy:

So what are the off vintages of the last 10 years?

None!

2016 should be at least very good to excellent
2015 outstanding
2014 excellent
2013 vg to excellent
2012 excellent
2011 mean to good, some drinking very well now
2010 outstanding
2009 outstanding (if you like the style)
2008 vg to excellent
2007 good, even sometimes very good, earlier drinkable
2006 good to excellent
2005 outstanding

(a very quick and rough personal take …)

2004 I would call partially “off” - depending on the “green meanies”
1997 is similar to 2007
1994 is quite weak, but the best are still drinking very well
1987/86 are quite weak (with few exceptions)
1984 is (now) off
posts.display_username: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
James Billy:

So what are the off vintages of the last 10 years?

The last off vintage in Burgundy is 2004. There are a lot of ugly 2004s. I would also consider 2003 to be an off vintage because there are so many weird wines from the vintage, although this is a strange one in that some of the successes of the vintage are truly fantastic.

All over the world, there are fewer off vintages than there used to be due to better ability to fight against rot and for other reasons.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
Gerhard P.:

[
1997 is similar to 2007

I would much prefer drinking a 2007 than a 1997 from most producers (Jadot did make very good 1997s). But stylistically, how do you think of the two vintages as being similar - I think of 1997 a being much riper than 2007. When a 1997 fails, it generally is for lack of elegance. When a 2007 fails, in my experience it is for lack of fruit.

posts.name: Fred C
posts.cooked:
James Billy:

So what are the off vintages of the last 10 years?

The only one I avoid is 11 (goes for the Chablis in 11 too). There is a certain green and stink to the few I have tried. I’d take 06 and 07 all day every day over 11 and have been for my backfilling needs since they are priced same or less.

posts.name: A Songeur
posts.cooked: Given Scarcity of availability at decent price, I would advise to wait for the wine to be mature before opening a bottle. I am grateful I now have a very small allocation and don’t intend to open any until ready and this applies to my unique bottle of 2006.

posts.name: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)
posts.cooked:
Howard Cooper:
Gerhard P.:

[
1997 is similar to 2007

I would much prefer drinking a 2007 than a 1997 from most producers (Jadot did make very good 1997s). But stylistically, how do you think of the two vintages as being similar - I think of 1997 a being much riper than 2007. When a 1997 fails, it generally is for lack of elegance. When a 2007 fails, in my experience it is for lack of fruit.

Just for instance I had a simply great bottle of 1997 RSV/Hudelot-Noellat recently … on the top-level the 1997s are perfect now … both vintages (97 and 07) were charming from the start, the best wines (1997) didn´t lack ripe fruit, they were balanced and did mature nicely over 15-20 years, they often show attractive sweetness, typicity and fine length and complexity (from good producers).
Sure there are differences, 2007 is slightly higher in acidity, some are a bit hollow on mid-palate, but most are not less ripe (and will ultimately balance out), and don´t forget that maturity is 10 years behind …

The major difference: it´s easier to find top-2007s … simply because there are more good proucers now …

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Nice note.

The sisters’ Clos Vougeot might well be my favorite red Burgundy grand cru if I had to chose. John Gilman sometimes compares it to Musigny and I can definitely see where he’s coming from.

I would also venture that the way it’s reviewed is a classic instance of perceived hierarchy influencing critics’ scoring. The Ruchottes-Chambertin is always served after the Clos Vougeot in the cellar, which is perfectly logical, since it is the more structural, ‘vertical’ and (generally) powerful wine. I don’t think, however, that it reflects quality; certainly not in every vintage. Yet it would be hard to point to many instances when a visiting writer has rated the Clos Vougeot above the Ruchottes.

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
William Kelley:

The sisters’ Clos Vougeot might well be my favorite red Burgundy grand cru if I had to chose. John Gilman sometimes compares it to Musigny and I can definitely see where he’s coming from.

I would also venture that the way it’s reviewed is a classic instance of perceived hierarchy influencing critics’ scoring. The Ruchottes-Chambertin is always served after the Clos Vougeot in the cellar, which is perfectly logical, since it is the more structural, ‘vertical’ and (generally) powerful wine. I don’t think, however, that it reflects quality; certainly not in every vintage. Yet it would be hard to point to many instances when a visiting writer has rated the Clos Vougeot above the Ruchottes.

The order of tasting…almost always leading to the producers’ own sentiments, is a quirky thing in Burgundy. But, it is mostly that, where it is not obvious. In the case of the Mugnerets, their father set that hierarchy a long time ago (He died 29 years ago…when I visited he was very sick.) He obviously valued the Ruchottes, which he bought with Charles Rousseau and another , non-winemaker (who has Christophe Roumier make the wine) more than the CV, which was the first wine Dr. Georges bought on his own…as his parents had to sell their CV holding during the Depression. The order of tasting sends a clear message of the owner’s feelings. And, in this case, particularly, the sisters have been reverential to their father’s views on many things…understandably.

A similar thing went on at Rousseau. Charles Rousseau’s first purchase on his own was his Clos St. Jacques. For some reason that usually escapes me, he valued it as lesser only to his Chambertin and Clos de Beze. That was not only reflected in the tasting order, but also the amount of new oak the CSJ got. (IMO it didn’t always absorb that NFO all that well). I always felt that the Mazy at Rousseau was an inherently better wine than the CSJ, as was, arguably, the Clos des Ruchottes, but … My guess is that Eric Rousseau will also defer to his father’s views, though…my sense is that he is less reverential than the Mugneret family has been.

Not surprisingly, since the professional tasters…taste in that order too, they almost always make their scoring follow the order of tasting. Why would they offend the host!!

There are other quirks at places, too…that I don’t necesssarily agree with…like the Cros P at Rouget/Jayer being tasted after the Echezaux, which I think is a more complete wine there. And, at Robert Chevillon, the Vaucrains is tasted last, after the les St. Georges…which is not an oversight.

Interesting.

And, FWIW, '06 is only IMO an “off” vintage in the sense that it followed '05, which most winemakers I talked to about it, believe is the greatest vintage ever…after 1990. 2006 is very nice. Certainly '04, a flawed vintage…is nowhere as good as "06.

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked:
c fu:

All over the world, there are fewer off vintages than there used to be due to better ability to fight against rot and for other reasons.

I’m not too happy with 2011 either. Other than that I agree.

I’ve had several really good 2011s recently:

Rollin Ile de V, Boillot Champonnets and a huge surprise Voillot Fremiets. The 2011s from Mugneret have also been quite good. This doesn’t have the same flaws as 2004. No other vintage around it will drink well on the younger side, so I think 2011s are extremely valuable. Like 2007.

Stuart, that’s an interesting point you make about professional critics being influenced by the order producers serve their wines. Some critics are just far too consistent up and down the hierarchy and across vintages when I compare their notes with my experiences. Some vineyards outperform in certain vintages but certain critics notes don’t always reflect this.

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked: I call these wine critics hierarchists–Clive Coates leading the pack. To them, grand cru is always better than 1er. Cros P seems an exception.

posts.name: A Songeur
posts.cooked:
Matthew,
I don’t know more than you when a wine is ready.
Critics and friends tell me that great bottles of grand cru (such as Clos Vougeot from Mugneret Gibourg) should get at least 15-20 years cellaring and given how I treasure this wine, I decided to comply, but as you enjoyed the wine, you were right to open it. I just wanted to make a general observation, not telling you you should have waited.

posts.name: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)
posts.cooked:
Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow:

…like the Cros P at Rouget/Jayer being tasted after the Echezaux, which I think is a more complete wine there. …

Stuart,
this is not my experience. Emmanuel served the Echezeaux AFTER the Cros Parantoux on two occasions … and while the CP was more powerful - the E. was more elegant and complex …
posts.display_username: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
This is really interesting, Gerhard. I just checked my notes and on every visit from 1996-2007 the Cros P. was priced by far the highest and always served last. Same for my only visit to Henri Jayer in 1992.

I stopped buying with the 2006 vintage…for several reasons and stopped visiting after 2007 (not happy about that…but…i’m not sure visiting and not buying would have worked out too well…I tell myself(

I wonder if Rouget changed things up…after Jayer died…or maybe presents differently to Austrians?

I did, candidly, tell him several times that I felt the Echezaux was a more complete wine, though I understood why he charged so much more for the Cros P. In fact, I once told him that, to my taste and wallet, the Beaux Monts was a better value and almost-equal wine.

Im not sure Rouget had ever tasted a Cros P in bottle…frankly. He never seemed all that interested in winetasting or older wines.


Do you think you can inquire about when there was a change and why your experience is so different?

posts.name: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)
posts.cooked:
Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow:

This is really interesting, Gerhard. I just checked my notes and on every visit from 1996-2007 the Cros P. was priced by far the highest and always served last. Same for my only visit to Henri Jayer in 1992.
…
Do you think you can inquire about when there was a change and why your experience is so different?

I visited there in 2008 and 2009 … and I´m very sure that the sequence was CP before E. - because I exspected it the other way round … and when asking for the reason, Emmanuel said that E. is Grand cru, and it is more elegant and complex (which was true) …

Since I wasn´t able to buy any bottles (and no allocation in sight, too) I didn´t ask for a visit later again … but maybe your opinion (in 2007?) has changed his mind - who knows …!?
